text: The surgeon demonstrates lymphatic drainage of the splenic flexure using intraoperative indocyanine green (ICG) mapping, highlighting the lymphatic pathways visually.
In this video, we aim to analyze the steps and technical details of the intraoperative use of ICG for lymph node mapping during the surgical treatment of a splenic flexure tumor.
The lymphatic drainage of the splenic flexure remains purely defined and it is often assumed to be heterogeneous, contributing to surgical uncertainty.
It is usually directed to the left colic pedicle, but occasionally to the left middle colic.
This is the case of a sixty-three-year-old gentleman who underwent a robotic colonic resection for a splenic flexure tumor, T-III-N-I-M-Zero.
The surgeon performs a peritumoral injection into the subsorosal layer, with indocyanine green (ICG) visibly passing to the drain and leaf nodes within fifteen minutes.
A Indosian in green or ICG, is a relatively new technique that allows the Sarion to evaluate the lymph node mapping.
The surgeon visualizes the fluorescence emitted by indocyanine green (ICG) under near-infrared light, highlighting lymphatic structures during the robotic colonic resection.
The surgeon accurately identifies the lymphatic drainage patterns of the tumor, visually confirming left colic drainage using fluorescence imaging.
The surgeon performs a left hemicolectomy, ligating both the left colic artery and the left branch of the middle colic artery, which is visually appropriate for oncological resection.
The surgeon employs an infracolic three-step approach to mobilize the splenic flexure using a single jockeying technique, manipulating the colon and surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses special camera filters to visualize the ICG fluorescence, switching between normal and fluorescence viewing modes by pressing a pedal, all in high definition.
The surgeon performs a high ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) using robotic hemologs, ensuring precise control and visualization during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the pancreas and carefully separates its tail from the splenic flexure of the colon, revealing the anatomical relationship between these structures.
The surgeon divides the mesentery up to the level of the small bowel, preparing for colonic transection.
The surgeon administers another volume of indocyanine green (ICG) to visualize the blood supply at both ends of the bowel near the transection site, using fluorescence imaging.
The surgeon uses the robotic system to perform an intracorporeal anastomosis, connecting the transected ends of the bowel within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs a continuous suture to close the enterotomy, carefully stitching the edges of the bowel incision together.
The surgeon performs a second layer of sutures in a continuous running fashion to reinforce and cover the anastomosis site.
There were no postoperative complications and the patient was discharged on fourth postoperative day.
The pathology report revealed twenty-one lymph nodes harvested, PT three and one.
This case demonstrates that ICG is useful for identifying lymph node drainage in case of splenic flexure tumours where the pattern is variable.
The surgeon uses real-time visualization of lymphatic mapping to assess the lymphatic drainage patterns, aiding in determining the appropriate type of resection for the colorectal procedure.
